title.zh: 祷过山上三足人面的白首怪鸟
title.en: Three-Legged, Human-Faced Bird of Mount Daoguo

summary.zh: 祷过山怪鸟，形状像池鹭一类的水鸟而头是白色，长着人的面孔和三只脚，叫声就是自己的名字。
summary.en: A bird of Mount Daoguo shaped like a small heron but white-headed, with a human face and three legs, forever calling out its own name.

description.zh:
《南山经》记祷过之山"有鸟焉，其状如䴔而白首，三足、人面，其名曰瞿如，其鸣自号也"。䴔是一种似凫的水鸟，旧注或以为即池鹭之属；瞿如像它，却顶着一颗白头，脸是人脸，脚有三只。祷过之山下多犀、兕与象，是全书少见的热带风物志，瞿如便栖在这片南国山林里。

"其鸣自号"——它叫出来的声音正是自己的名字，仿佛一头会自报家门的鸟。这是《山海经》命名学里一个反复出现的程式：南次二经柜山的鴸"其名自号"，令丘山的颙"其鸣自号"，都属此类。古人为陌生的鸟兽命名，最省事的办法就是摹写其叫声，经文却把因果倒转，说成鸟在呼喊自己的名字，于是每一声鸟鸣都成了自我介绍。瞿如没有附带功效，也不预示灾祥，在通篇"食之""佩之""见则"的账目里，它罕见地只是被看见、被记下——三只脚站在水边，白着头，喊自己的名字。
description.en:
Mount Daoguo, whose lower slopes teem with rhinoceroses, buffalo, and elephants — one of the book's rare glimpses of tropical fauna — is also home to the quru. The Classic of the Southern Mountains describes a bird shaped like the jiao, a duck-like waterbird that old commentators identify with small herons, but with a white head, a human face, and three legs. Its cry, the text adds, is its own name.

That last clause follows a recurring formula in the book's science of names. The zhu of Mount Ju 'calls its own name'; so does the drought-bird Yong of Mount Lingqiu. The practical origin is plain — the easiest way to name an unknown bird is to transcribe its call — but the text reverses cause and effect, so that the bird seems to announce itself, every cry a self-introduction. Unusually, the quru comes with no prescription and no portent: in a ledger otherwise ruled by 'eat it and', 'wear it and', 'when seen', it is simply observed and recorded — a white-headed thing on three legs at the water's edge, shouting its own name.

value.en: White head, human face, three legs
Its call is its own name
